"Then Jacob went on his journey, and came into the land of the people of the east." The witnesses note Jacob continuing his journey to the land of the east. On "Jacob went on his journey," the grammatical voices note the encouragement. Ellicott and Gill note that "Jacob went on his journey" — the Hebrew ("Jacob lifted up his feet") suggests that, after the vision and the promise at Bethel (28:12-15), Jacob went forward with renewed cheer and lightness of step; the comfort of the covenant-God speeding the pilgrim on; the encouraged traveler.

On "lifted up his feet," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the cheer. Henry and Calvin note the lively phrase — Jacob, assured of God's presence (28:15), proceeded with courage and gladness; the comfort of the vision bearing him up; the pilgrim strengthened for the way.

On "came into the land of the people of the east," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the arrival: "came into the land of the people of the east" — Jacob reached the region of Mesopotamia/Padan-aram (the lands east of Canaan, the kindred, 28:2); the providence bringing him to the place of his kindred; the setting for the meeting at the well (vv.2-14). The verse displays Jacob continuing his journey to the land of the east.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the journey — "Jacob went on his journey," the encouraged pilgrim (28:15, Ellicott, Gill); "lifted up his feet," the cheer after Bethel (Henry, Calvin); "came into the land of the people of the east," the arrival in the kindred's land (28:2, Calvin, grammatical-historical). "And he looked, and behold a well in the field, and, lo, there were three flocks of sheep lying by it; for out of that well they watered the flocks: and a great stone was upon the well's mouth." Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "behold a well in the field, and, lo, there were three flocks of sheep lying by it; for out of that well they watered the flocks" — Jacob came upon a well in the field, with three flocks waiting to be watered; the well a place of meeting (as for Abraham's servant, 24:11); the pastoral scene; the setting for the meeting with Rachel (v.6).

On "a great stone was upon the well's mouth," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the stone. Clarke and Ellicott note "a great stone was upon the well's mouth" — a heavy stone covering the well (to protect the water, and to keep it from being used until all the flocks gathered, v.8); the stone Jacob would roll away (v.10); the custom of the place.

On the well, the reformed and grammatical-historical note the providence: the well, the flocks, and the stone set the scene for the providential meeting (vv.9-12) — Jacob brought to the very well where Rachel would come; the providence ordering the encounter (as with Rebekah, ch. 24); the setting for the founding of the family. The verse displays Jacob finding the well with the great stone.

On "they rolled the stone... and watered the sheep," the witnesses note the labor. Gill notes the rolling of the heavy stone — requiring the gathered shepherds; the protection of the well; the communal arrangement.

Calvin and Gill note that Laban offered "this also" (Rachel) after Leah's week — Jacob to have both sisters (the bigamy, two wives in eight days, v.28); the offer that bound Jacob to a second term; the two sisters married (the source of the rivalry, chs. 30); the marriage not to be judged by the later law (Leviticus 18:18, the witnesses note), there being then no positive law against it, yet not justified.

On "yet seven other years," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the second service: "for the service which thou shalt serve with me yet seven other years" — Laban exacted a second seven years' service for Rachel; the shrewd bargainer gaining fourteen years of Jacob's labor; the covetous Laban (chs. 30-31) profiting by Jacob; the long service for the beloved wife. The verse displays Laban's offer of Rachel for a second seven years.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the offer — "Fulfil her week, and we will give thee this also... yet seven other years," Rachel for a second term (Judges 14:12, Ellicott, Gill); "we will give thee this also," the second wife, the bigamy (Leviticus 18:18, Calvin, Gill); "yet seven other years," the second service (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the bargaining Laban. The verse marks the week: of Leah. The verse displays the Rachel: also given. The verse displays the service: seven more years. The verse displays the gain: Laban's. The offer displays Laban gaining a second term of service. The verse displays the Laban: offering. The verse displays the Rachel: promised. The verse displays the service: doubled. Laban's offer of Rachel for a second seven years displays the shrewd bargain: fulfil Leah's week, and have Rachel also for seven other years (Judges 14:12) — Laban gaining fourteen years of Jacob's labor, the two sisters married (the source of the rivalry, chs. 30). The verse displays the bargaining Laban, accepted by Jacob (v.28).
28:
"And Jacob did so, and fulfilled her week: and he gave him Rachel his daughter to wife also." The witnesses note Jacob fulfilling the week and receiving Rachel. On "Jacob did so, and fulfilled her week," the grammatical voices note the compliance. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Jacob did so, and fulfilled her week: and he gave him Rachel his daughter to wife also" — Jacob accepted Laban's terms (v.27), completed Leah's marriage-week, and received Rachel also; the patriarch yielding to the situation (bound by the fraud, and by his love for Rachel); the two sisters now both his wives (within eight days); the bigamy accomplished.

On "fulfilled her week," the witnesses note the wedding-week. Gill notes that Jacob completed Leah's seven-day wedding feast (v.27) before taking Rachel; the honoring of the week; the second marriage following.

On "he gave him Rachel his daughter to wife also," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the second wife: "he gave him Rachel his daughter to wife also" — Rachel given as a second wife (the beloved, v.30); the two sisters married to Jacob; the source of the rivalry and the gathering of the tribes (chs. 30); the bigamy (not justified, yet overruled by God to multiply the seed, the witnesses note). The verse displays Jacob fulfilling the week and receiving Rachel.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the compliance — "Jacob did so, and fulfilled her week: and he gave him Rachel," Jacob completing the week and receiving Rachel (v.27, Keil, Gill); "fulfilled her week," the wedding-week honored (Gill); "he gave him Rachel his daughter to wife also," the second wife (v.30, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the complying Jacob. The verse marks the week: fulfilled. The verse displays the Rachel: received. The verse displays the wives: two. The verse displays the rivalry: to come. The compliance displays Jacob receiving both sisters. The verse displays the Jacob: fulfilling. The verse displays the Rachel: his wife. The verse displays the bigamy: accomplished. Jacob fulfilling the week and receiving Rachel displays the two sisters married to him: Jacob completed Leah's week and received Rachel also (v.27) — the beloved Rachel his second wife, the two sisters the source of the rivalry and the gathering of the tribes (chs. 30). The verse displays the married Jacob, with Bilhah given to Rachel (v.29).
29:
"And Laban gave to Rachel his daughter Bilhah his handmaid to be her maid." The very brief verse notes Laban giving Bilhah to Rachel. The grammatical voices note the handmaid. Gill notes that "Laban gave to Rachel his daughter Bilhah his handmaid to be her maid" — Laban gave Rachel a maidservant, Bilhah (as he had given Zilpah to Leah, v.24); the customary wedding-gift of a handmaid (24:59); Bilhah who would later bear Jacob sons (Dan and Naphtali, 30:3-8); the handmaid given.

On "Bilhah his handmaid," the witnesses note the maid. Gill notes that Bilhah was given to Rachel — the maidservant of the beloved wife; the handmaid who would bear two of the twelve sons (30:3-8); the customary provision.

On the gift, the grammatical-historical note the providence: Bilhah, given to Rachel, would (with Zilpah, v.24) become a mother of the tribes (Dan, Naphtali, 30:3-8); the providence gathering the four mothers (Leah, Rachel, Zilpah, Bilhah) whose sons would be the twelve tribes of Israel; the household of Jacob being formed. The verse displays Laban giving Bilhah to Rachel.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the gift — "Laban gave to Rachel his daughter Bilhah his handmaid," the handmaid given (v.24, Gill); "Bilhah his handmaid," the maid of Rachel (Gill); the mother of Dan and Naphtali (30:3-8, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the giving Laban. The verse marks the maid: Bilhah. The verse displays the Rachel: her handmaid. The verse displays the gift: customary. The verse displays the tribes: to come. The gift displays the handmaid provided for Rachel. Thus, within a few days, the household of Jacob was fully constituted -- two wives and two handmaids -- the four women through whom, in the providence of God, the twelve sons and the twelve tribes of Israel would be born (Genesis 35:22-26), the small beginnings of the nation promised to Abraham (Genesis 28:14). The verse displays the Laban: giving. The verse displays the Bilhah: to Rachel. The verse displays the providence: gathering. Laban giving Bilhah to Rachel displays the gathering of the mothers of the tribes: Laban gave Rachel Bilhah for a handmaid (v.24) — the maidservant who would bear Dan and Naphtali (30:3-8), the four mothers of the twelve tribes being gathered. The verse displays the married Jacob, loving Rachel more (v.30).
30:
"And he went in also unto Rachel, and he loved also Rachel more than Leah, and served with him yet seven other years." The witnesses note Jacob loving Rachel more and serving a second seven years. On "he went in also unto Rachel, and he loved also Rachel more than Leah," the grammatical voices note the preference. Gill and Calvin note that "he went in also unto Rachel, and he loved also Rachel more than Leah" — Jacob took Rachel also to wife and loved her more than Leah; the preference for the beloved Rachel (the beautiful, v.17, for whom he had served); the favoritism that would breed the rivalry and Leah's grief (vv.31-35); the divided affection (as Isaac and Rebekah's, 25:28).

On "loved also Rachel more than Leah," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note Leah's grief. Calvin and Gill note that Jacob's loving Rachel more left Leah less loved (hated, v.31, i.e., loved less) — the source of Leah's sorrow; the bitter fruit of the bigamy and the deception; the rivalry of the sisters; the unloved Leah whom God would regard (v.31).

On "served with him yet seven other years," the reformed and grammatical-historical note the second service: "served with him yet seven other years" — Jacob served the second seven years for Rachel (v.27); the long labor (fourteen years in all, for the two wives); the diligent service under the covetous Laban (chs. 30-31); the patriarch's toil for his family. The verse displays Jacob loving Rachel more and serving a second seven years.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the preference — "he loved also Rachel more than Leah," the favored Rachel (v.17, Gill, Calvin); the less-loved Leah (v.31, Calvin, Gill); "served with him yet seven other years," the second service (v.27, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the preferring Jacob. The verse marks the Rachel: loved more. The verse displays the Leah: less loved. The verse displays the service: seven more years. The verse displays the rivalry: to come. The preference displays Jacob's divided affection. The verse displays the Jacob: loving Rachel. The verse displays the Leah: grieved. The verse displays the service: long. Jacob loving Rachel more and serving a second seven years display the favored wife and the long labor: Jacob loved Rachel more than Leah and served seven other years (v.27) — the preference that grieved the less-loved Leah (v.31), the fourteen years' service for the two wives. The verse displays the divided household, with the LORD's regard for Leah (v.31).
31:
"And when the LORD saw that Leah was hated, he opened her womb: but Rachel was barren." The witnesses note the LORD opening Leah's womb because she was hated — the crux of God's regard for the unloved. On "when the LORD saw that Leah was hated," interpreters note the meaning of "hated." The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note that "the LORD saw that Leah was hated" — "hated" here means loved less (the comparative, as v.30, loved less than Rachel; the Hebrew idiom, cf. Luke 14:26; Malachi 1:3); Leah was the unloved, slighted wife; and the LORD "saw" her affliction (the slighted, sorrowful wife); the eye of God on the despised; God's notice of the unloved.

On "he opened her womb," interpreters expound God's compassion. The dominant reading notes that God "opened her womb" — gave Leah children (while Rachel, the loved, was barren); the tender compassion of God toward the unloved and afflicted — that the despised wife was regarded and comforted by the LORD with the blessing of children; the divine redress of the imbalance (the loved wife barren, the unloved fruitful); the providence that comforted Leah and humbled the favored Rachel; the LORD's care for the slighted (Psalm 113:7-9; the witnesses note that "the despised and afflicted of men are regarded by the Lord").

On "but Rachel was barren," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the contrast: "but Rachel was barren" — the beloved Rachel was childless (for a time); the contrast (the loved barren, the unloved fruitful) displaying God's sovereign, gracious ordering — comforting the afflicted Leah, and (in time) teaching Rachel to seek God (30:1, 22); the providence balancing the household; the mercy to the unloved. The verse displays the LORD opening Leah's womb because she was hated.

The witnesses agree the substance: because Leah was loved less, the LORD gave her children while Rachel was barren (Calvin, Wesley, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of God's regard for the unloved: "hated" meaning loved less (v.30; Luke 14:26); the LORD's seeing and compassionating the slighted, sorrowful Leah, and comforting her with children (while the loved Rachel was barren) — the tender mercy of God toward the despised and afflicted (Psalm 113:7-9), the sovereign redress that regarded the unloved. The verse displays the regard-for-the-unloved crux. The verse marks the hated: loved less. The verse displays the LORD: who saw. The verse displays the womb: opened. The verse displays the mercy: to the unloved. The regard displays God comforting the slighted Leah. The verse displays the Leah: regarded. The verse displays the LORD: compassionate. The verse displays the Rachel: barren. The LORD opening Leah's womb because she was hated displays the crux of God's regard for the unloved: the LORD saw Leah was hated (loved less, v.30) and opened her womb, while Rachel was barren — the tender compassion of God toward the slighted and afflicted (Psalm 113:7-9), comforting the unloved with children. The verse displays the regarded Leah, who bears Reuben (v.32).
32:
"And Leah conceived, and bare a son, and she called his name Reuben: for she said, Surely the LORD hath looked upon my affliction; now therefore my husband will love me." The witnesses note the birth of Reuben and Leah's hope of her husband's love. On "Leah conceived, and bare a son, and she called his name Reuben," the grammatical voices note the name. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Leah... bare a son, and she called his name Reuben: for she said, Surely the LORD hath looked upon my affliction" — Leah bore her firstborn and named him Reuben ("see, a son," or "the LORD hath looked"); the name commemorating that the LORD had looked upon her affliction (her being unloved, v.31); the acknowledgment of God's notice; the gratitude of the regarded wife.

On "Surely the LORD hath looked upon my affliction," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the affliction. Calvin and Gill note that Leah owned "the LORD hath looked upon my affliction" — God had seen her sorrow (the slighted wife, v.31); the comfort of God's notice; the faith that ascribed the child to the LORD's compassion; the regarded affliction.

On "now therefore my husband will love me," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the hope: "now therefore my husband will love me" — Leah hoped that, having borne a son, Jacob would now love her; the craving of the unloved wife for her husband's affection; the natural desire (yet still looking to the husband's love); the hope that would not be fully met (and would turn, at last, to praising God, v.35). The verse displays the birth of Reuben and Leah's hope.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the name — "she called his name Reuben: for she said, Surely the LORD hath looked upon my affliction," the LORD's notice (v.31, Keil, Gill); "the LORD hath looked upon my affliction," God's compassion owned (Calvin, Gill); "now therefore my husband will love me," the craving for love (v.35, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the bearing Leah. The verse marks the son: Reuben. The verse displays the affliction: looked upon. The verse displays the hope: my husband will love me. The verse displays the LORD: regarding. The birth displays Leah comforted yet craving love. The verse displays the Leah: bearing. The verse displays the Reuben: named. The verse displays the hope: for love. The birth of Reuben and Leah's hope display the regarded affliction and the craving for love: Leah bore Reuben, for the LORD looked on her affliction; now will my husband love me (v.31) — the unloved wife comforted by God's notice, yet still craving Jacob's love. The verse displays the bearing Leah, who bears Simeon (v.33).
33:
"And she conceived again, and bare a son; and said, Because the LORD hath heard that I was hated, he hath therefore given me this son also: and she called his name Simeon." The very brief verse notes the birth of Simeon, the LORD hearing Leah was hated. The grammatical voices note the name. Gill notes that "she conceived again, and bare a son; and said, Because the LORD hath heard that I was hated, he hath therefore given me this son also: and she called his name Simeon" — Leah bore a second son and named him Simeon ("hearing," from the LORD's hearing); the name commemorating that the LORD had heard she was hated (loved less, v.31); the acknowledgment of God's hearing; the comfort of the regarded wife.

On "the LORD hath heard that I was hated," the witnesses note God's hearing. Gill notes that Leah owned "the LORD hath heard that I was hated" — God had heard her affliction (the slighted wife); the comfort of God's hearing (as Reuben commemorated his looking, v.32); the faith ascribing the son to the LORD; the despised wife regarded by the God who hears.

On "she called his name Simeon," the grammatical-historical note the comfort: "she called his name Simeon (hearing)" — the second son a token of God's hearing; the unloved wife comforted again; yet still (as Reuben, v.32) the longing for her husband's love mingled with the gratitude; the regarded Leah, the God who hears the afflicted (Psalm 34:6). The verse displays the birth of Simeon.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the name — "the LORD hath heard that I was hated... she called his name Simeon," the LORD's hearing (v.31, Gill); "the LORD hath heard that I was hated," God's notice of the slighted (Gill); "Simeon (hearing)," the comfort (Psalm 34:6, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the bearing Leah. The verse marks the son: Simeon. The verse displays the hearing: of the LORD. The verse displays the hated: heard. The verse displays the comfort: again. The birth displays Leah comforted by God's hearing. The verse displays the Leah: bearing. The verse displays the Simeon: named. The verse displays the LORD: who hears. The birth of Simeon displays the LORD hearing Leah was hated: Leah bore Simeon, for the LORD heard she was hated (v.31) — the unloved wife comforted again by the God who hears the afflicted (Psalm 34:6). The verse displays the bearing Leah, who bears Levi (v.34).
34:
"And she conceived again, and bare a son; and said, Now this time will my husband be joined unto me, because I have born him three sons: therefore was his name called Levi." The witnesses note the birth of Levi and Leah's hope of being joined to her husband. On "Now this time will my husband be joined unto me, because I have born him three sons," the grammatical voices note the hope. Gill and Clarke note that "Now this time will my husband be joined unto me, because I have born him three sons" — Leah bore a third son and hoped that now, having borne three sons, Jacob would be joined (attached, knit in affection) to her; the continued craving of the unloved wife for her husband's love; the hope renewed with each son; the longing for the marriage-bond of affection.

On "my husband be joined unto me," the witnesses note the longing. Gill notes the longing — that Jacob would be "joined" (the word from which "Levi" is derived) to her in love; the unloved wife still seeking her husband's affection; the hope (still set on the husband) renewed.

On "therefore was his name called Levi," the grammatical-historical note the name: "his name called Levi (joined)" — the third son named Levi, from the hope of being joined to Jacob; the name commemorating the longing; (and Levi, the priestly tribe, the line of the priesthood, Exodus 32:26-29); the son named in hope. The verse displays the birth of Levi and Leah's hope of being joined to her husband.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the hope — "Now this time will my husband be joined unto me... therefore was his name called Levi," the longing for the husband's love (Gill, Clarke); "my husband be joined unto me," the craving (Gill); "Levi (joined)," the name of hope (the priestly tribe, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the hoping Leah. The verse marks the son: Levi. The verse displays the hope: joined to Jacob. The verse displays the three sons: borne. The verse displays the longing: for love. The birth displays Leah still craving her husband's love. The verse displays the Leah: bearing. The verse displays the Levi: named. The verse displays the hope: for union. The birth of Levi and Leah's hope of being joined to her husband display the renewed longing: now will my husband be joined to me, for I have borne three sons; his name Levi (joined) — the unloved wife still craving Jacob's love, the son named in hope (Levi, the priestly tribe). The verse displays the hoping Leah, who at last turns to praise (v.35).
35:
"And she conceived again, and bare a son: and she said, Now will I praise the LORD: therefore she called his name Judah; and left bearing." The witnesses note the birth of Judah and Leah's turning to praise — the crux of "now will I praise the LORD." On "she said, Now will I praise the LORD," interpreters note the turning of Leah's heart. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke and the dominant reading note that, at the birth of her fourth son, Leah said, "Now will I praise the LORD" — a marked turning: with the first three sons, Leah's words still looked to her husband's love (vv.32-34, "now will my husband love me... be joined to me"); but now, at the fourth, she rises above the craving for human affection to pure praise of God — "now will I praise the LORD"; the heart at last fixed on God rather than on her husband's love; the lesson that the soul finds rest not in human love but in praising God; the turning from self to praise.

On "therefore she called his name Judah," interpreters expound the name. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note that Leah "called his name Judah" — Judah ("praise," from the resolve to praise the LORD, yehudah, a confession/praise); and the witnesses mark the great significance: Judah was the tribe of the kings (David, and the line of the Messiah, springing from him, Matthew 1:2-3; Hebrews 7:14, "our Lord sprang out of Judah"; the Lion of the tribe of Judah, Revelation 5:5); the providence that gave the royal and Messianic line through Leah's fourth son, named "praise"; the unloved wife the mother of the line of Christ; "the Messiah springing from him, which gave occasion of joy and praise."

On "and left bearing," the grammatical-historical note the pause: "and left bearing" — Leah ceased (for a time) from bearing (resuming later, 30:17-21); the pause after the four sons; the close of this first round of births; Leah the mother of Reuben, Simeon, Levi, and Judah (a third of the tribes, including the royal and priestly lines). The verse displays the birth of Judah and Leah's turning to praise.

The witnesses agree the substance: at Judah's birth, Leah turned from craving her husband's love to praising the LORD, and named him Judah (Clarke, Calvin, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the turning to praise: Leah, who with the first three sons looked to her husband's love (vv.32-34), now rises to pure praise of God ("now will I praise the LORD"), the heart fixed on God rather than human affection; and Judah ("praise") the tribe of the kings and the line of the Messiah (Matthew 1:2-3; Hebrews 7:14; Revelation 5:5) — the unloved wife the mother of the line of Christ. The verse displays the praise crux. The verse marks the son: Judah. The verse displays the turning: to praise the LORD. The verse displays the Messiah: of Judah's line. The verse displays the heart: fixed on God. The praise displays Leah rising from craving love to praising God. The verse displays the Leah: praising. The verse displays the Judah: named. The verse displays the Christ: of his tribe. The birth of Judah and Leah's turning to praise display the crux: now will I praise the LORD; his name Judah — Leah rising from the craving for her husband's love (vv.32-34) to pure praise of God, and Judah ("praise") the tribe of the kings and the line of the Messiah (Hebrews 7:14; Revelation 5:5), the unloved wife the mother of the line of Christ. The verse closes the chapter, before the rivalry over children (ch.30).
